entry.title: What Is SAP Enterprise Service Management and Why It Matters Now
entry.content:
Service expectations have changed.

An employee needs help with payroll. A supplier has a billing question. A partner needs fast approval. Each one expects the same speed and clarity they get as consumers. When service lives in separate tools and teams, delays follow and frustration grows.

That’s where SAP Enterprise Service Management (ESM) fits.

SAP Enterprise Service Management helps organizations deliver fast, consistent service across the enterprise not just for customers, but for employees, suppliers, and partners too.

 

What Is SAP Enterprise Service Management?

SAP Enterprise Service Management is SAP’s enterprise-wide, omnichannel service solution. It standardizes how service is delivered across areas like HR, finance, procurement, facilities, and shared services.

Instead of juggling siloed ticketing tools, teams work from one AI-enabled experience from inquiry to resolution. The result is clearer ownership, faster outcomes, and lower operational effort.

 

Is SAP Enterprise Service Management Part of SAP Service Cloud Version 2?

This question comes up often. The short answer is yes and no.

SAP Enterprise Service Management is built on the same modern foundation as SAP Service Cloud Version 2, but it serves a different purpose.

Both solutions share:

  • A modern case management framework
  • A unified agent workspace
  • Embedded AI
  • Native Microsoft Teams integration
  • Deep integration with SAP back-end systems

Where they differ is who they serve.

  • SAP Service Cloud Version 2 focuses on external customer service (B2B and B2C).
  • SAP Enterprise Service Management supports internal and enterprise services for employees, suppliers, partners, and shared service teams.

Many organizations run both side by side. Together, they create one service operating model inside and out.

 

Is SAP Enterprise Service Management a New Solution?

SAP Enterprise Service Management isn’t an experiment. It’s the next step in SAP's approach to enterprise service delivery.

SAP has supported HR and shared services for years. ESM is the first solution designed from the start to manage enterprise-wide service at scale.

It includes:

  • Pre-delivered service scenarios
  • Configurable case models
  • AI-driven automation
  • Tight integration with SAP S/4HANA, SAP SuccessFactors, and other SAP solutions

We continue to invest heavily in ESM, with quarterly innovation and growing adoption, including across SAP’s own internal service teams.

 

How SAP Enterprise Service Management Works with SAP CX

SAP Enterprise Service Management extends the SAP Customer Experience portfolio. It doesn’t replace it.

Together, SAP CX and ESM support:

  • End-to-end service orchestration across front and back office
  • Consistent experiences for customers and employees
  • Shared insights across service domains
  • Reuse of AI, automation, and workflows

For example, a customer case in SAP Service Cloud Version 2 can trigger a finance or procurement request handled in SAP ESM. Agents work in familiar tools, while users access services through simple self-service entry points.

The outcome is scale without creating new silos.

 

How Organizations Benefit from SAP Enterprise Service Management

Customers typically see value in four areas.

Centralized Service Delivery

Enterprise inquiries across HR, finance, IT, and procurement are managed in one system. Visibility improves, and handoffs become easier.

Faster Resolution with AI

Embedded AI supports case classification, routing, summarization, and knowledge recommendations. Teams spend less time on manual work and close cases faster.

Better Employee and Partner Experiences

Self-service portals, guided forms, and omnichannel access reduce friction and increase satisfaction for everyone involved.

Lower Total Cost of Ownership

Pre-delivered content and native SAP integrations reduce custom development and speed time to value.

 

Get Started with the SAP Enterprise Service Management TKB

If you want to explore further, the SAP Enterprise Service Management Technical Knowledge Base (TKB) is a strong starting point.

The TKB helps you:

  • Understand solution positioning and value
  • Review implementation guidance and best practices
  • Explore architecture and integration options
  • Stay current with innovations and releases

It supports the full journey from discovery to optimization.

Final Thoughts

SAP Enterprise Service Management helps organizations rethink service. Instead of disconnected tickets, service becomes an intelligent, connected experience across the enterprise.

Built on the same foundation as SAP Service Cloud Version 2 and aligned with SAP CX, ESM supports faster, smarter, and more consistent service wherever it happens.

If you’re ready to extend service excellence beyond customer support and into the core of the business, SAP Enterprise Service Management is designed to help you get there.

entry.title: Re: Can I simulate actions in JS for external services for complex back-end logic?
entry.content:
Hi Martin,

This quick example is very basic (and note - if I create json files, the service mocks this fine):

extending the admin-service.cds from the sample:

using {sap.capire.bookshop as my} from '../db/schema';
using {API_BUSINESS_PARTNER as external} from './external/API_BUSINESS_PARTNER';
service AdminService {
  entity Customer as projection on external.A_BusinessPartner;
  entity Authors as projection on my.Authors;
  entity Books   as projection on my.Books;
  entity Genres  as projection on my.Genres;
}

Note - I don't want to modify the admin-service.js file for this service, as this is about a mock framework to run in development mode, so code should be identical to production code. 

That said, possibly my mock.js is the issue as it looks like this (and just trying to figure if it works yet rather than do anything) - e.g. It could be trying to load it, maybe needing the cds.connect.to('API_BUSINESS_PARTNER') - and is that what you are referring to?

const cds = require('@sap/cds');

module.exports = cds.service.impl(async function() {
	const { A_BusinessPartner } = this.entities;

	this.on('READ', A_BusinessPartner, request => {
		return null;
	});
});

 Thanks,

Matt

entry.title: Get XML Data for Local Print Form Preview in SAP Sales/Service Cloud Version 2
entry.content:
Overview of my Print Form Resources

I decided to create a separate article to avoid duplicate content in each print form blog post and simplify the maintenance. You can find the list of print form-related Blogs, information on Live Sessions and an Onboarding Video for beginners in the following blog post:

Print Form Adaptation with Adobe LiveCycle Designer Overview - Onboarding, Blogs and Live Session

 

Intro

Previewing print forms "locally" within the Adobe LiveCycle Designer is an essential feature. Otherwise, you spend too much time uploading the print form to the system to preview it online after every small change. However, not only changing the template and testing it straight away is a significant advantage. Simply adapting the XML sample data to see, for instance, how a field looks when containing a different value, longer text, etc., is beneficial in contrast to creating a new sample document within the system for this purpose.

If you are looking for details on this topic for SAP Sales/Service Cloud version 1 (formerly known as "Cloud for Customer"), you should have a look at this blog post: How to get XML data for local preview of print forms without output history.

 

REST API Approach

SAP Sales/Service Cloud Version 2 heavily relies on REST APIs, for instance, to load object data in the web frontend. You can see this in the browser's Developer Tools / Network monitor. In general, the object structure provided by these services is compatible with the form template sample data. For Sales Quotes, there is a special parameter that returns additional data (pricing elements with pre-calculated totals) for the output.

There are two challenges:

  1. Find the right service endpoint and object id
  2. The response of the REST API is in JSON format, whereas the Adobe LiveCycle Designer requires XML

I'll solve both challenges in this blog.

 

Tools - Prerequisites

As part of the procedure, we must convert JSON to XML. This is also mentioned in the SAP Note / Knowledgebase Article 3620996 (Sales Quote XML – How to Retrieve JSON Data for Conversion in Sales Cloud Version 2).
In this guide, I use Visual Studio Code (VSCode) in combination with plugin:

  •  convert json, yaml, xml, and others (petli-full.json-to-yaml-and-more)

Visual Studio Code is free and available for all common platforms. The mentioned plugins are published under the MIT license, and their source code can be found on Git Hub.
Never use any kind of free or publicly available online services for such kinds of tasks due to data protection and privacy reasons!

 

 

Procedure

Log in to SAP Sales/Service Cloud Version 2 with your browser and navigate to the screen from where you can open the desired business object.

Browser Developer Tools

Open your browser's Developer Tools and activate the Network tab. In most browsers they look very similar and offer the same feature set. Depending on your browser, some elements might have a different caption. The screenshots of this blog were created using the Windows version of Google Chrome. In many browsers you can also access the developer tools by pressing [F12] or the key combination [Ctrl]+[Shift]+[ i ].

In Chrome, there are two buttons on the very left (A) that allow you to start and stop recording network activities and clear the log in case you have recorded too much and want to start over again. Make sure the recording is active while carrying out the next step.

Find the right URL

In SAP Sales/Service Cloud Version 2, you can now hit the button to generate the PDF. In the sales quotes, it's called Preview and can be found in the three-dots menu. In other objects, it might be called differently, such as Generate Summary, etc.

After that, the network monitor reveals the actual URL we need:

  • For the sales quotes, it'll show two calls: call with a UUID followed by ?$forOutput=true and a preview.
    Copy the URL with forOutput=true as it is.
  • For other objects, it'll only show preview. The full URL also contains a UUID and ends with /preview.
    Copy the URL of this call without the /preview at the end.

The following two screenshots show examples for sales quotes and leads.

Screenshot for Sales Quotes:

 

Screenshot for Leads:

Alternatively, you activate the network monitor right before you open the object. Then, you have to search for the call to the object itself (with its UUID) instead of a preview call. That means, when opening a lead in SAP Sales/Service Cloud Version 2, the Network monitor will show a call to the service /sap/c4c/api/v1/lead-service/leads/<UUID> that returns the lead data as JSON.

Retrieve the JSON Data

Paste the copied URL into the address bar of your browser. The browser will show a JSON document. (It's important that you're logged in to SAP Sales/Service Cloud Version 2 when accessing the copied URL. In case you have been logged out, for instance due to inactivity, the call will fail. You won't be redirected to a login screen.

 

Convert JSON to XML

Copy the JSON text into a new file in Visual Studio Code. Then, open the command palette and search for JSON, YAML, XML and more. After selecting it, you have to enter the source and the target format in a second prompt. Enter "json | xml" (without the quotes) and wait a moment. The plugin will automatically create the XML representation of the JSON file in a new document tab.

The final step is to replace the root element <root> and the XML declaration with the one for Adobe form templates:

<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?>
<xfa:data xmlns:xfa="http://www.xfa.org/schema/xfa-data/1.0/">
	<data>
		...
	</data>
</xfa:data>

The following screenshot shows the XML before (left) and after (right):

After replacing the root element, you have the final sample data XML that you can load into the Adobe LiveCycle Designer for local preview.

That's it!

At first, this procedure might seem a bit complex and cumbersome. But it's worth the effort! The good news is: You can do it whenever you need it without opening a support ticket and waiting for an answer, or uploading a special template, which might not be possible in a productive environment, etc.

 

Limitations

When comparing the data from the REST API response with the data that is actually used for rendering PDFs, I found out that there are differences. In my test, the REST API was missing some fields (for instance, for the sales quote: formatted address fields). In addition to that, some number/currency fields used a different number formatting.

 

Conclusion

While the data of the REST API might serve as valid XML sample data from the structural point of view, it might still differ. Hence, you cannot completely rely on this approach for the entire design and testing process, at least not without knowing the differences and where to pay attention to. However, knowing these differences can still help you quickly get sample data even if it is out of productive systems.

If you want to be sure you're looking at the exact same data as the Adobe Document Services receive when rendering the form, you should consider using my Debug Sheet, which can be found in my other blog post: DebugSheet: Get XML Data for Local Print Form Preview in the Adobe LiveCycle Designer.

entry.title: Snowflake Adapter Operations with Optional Parameters
entry.content:
Snowflake is a modern cloud-based data platform that allows you to store, process, and analyze large amounts of data efficiently. It is widely used for data warehousing, analytics, and data integration. 

In SAP Integration Suite (CPI), the Snowflake Adapter helps you connect to Snowflake and perform operations like: 

  • Data loading 
  • Querying 
  • Data export 

To get started with Snowflake Adapter, refer to the Snowflake Introductory Blog. 

While these operations work out of the box, using optional parameters can significantly improve performance, cost, and reliability. 

Why Do We Need Optional Parameters? 

In real-world iFlows, you often deal with: 

  • Large files 
  • Multiple file uploads 
  • Error handling 
  • Storage optimization 

This is where optional parameters become very useful. 

Optional parameters help you to: 

  • Control how data is loaded/unloaded 
  • Improve performance 
  • Reduce storage costs 
  • Handle errors gracefully 

The latest version (1.2.0) of the adapter also supports bulk load/unload using Snowflake Internal Stage with optional parameters. 

Let us explore various scenarios where optional parameters can be used in your day to day processes. 

SCENARIO 1: Handling reprocessing and errors while uploading a file 

Problem: A file is uploaded into Snowflake and sometimes the same file may be sent again, or a few records inside it may have errors. You want the load to continue without failing completely despite a few bad rows or the file was already processed before.

Parameters Used: 

  • FORCE = TRUE 
  • ON_ERROR = CONTINUE 

What Happens: 

  • FORCE = TRUE → Loads files even if they were already processed 
  • ON_ERROR = CONTINUE → Skips bad records and continues processing 

Why Use This: 

  • Prevents pipeline failure due to a few bad rows. 
  • Useful in real-time or bulk integrations where data quality may vary. 

SCENARIO 2: Purging staged file in Snowflake after BulkUpsert load 

Problem: After BulkUpsert loads data from stage files into Snowflake tables, the files remain in the stage area unless they are removed. Over time, these leftover files build up, use extra storage, and may accidentally be picked up again for processing. 

Parameter Used: 

  • PURGE = TRUE 

What Happens: 

  • Deletes files from the stage after successful load 

Why Use This: 

  • Saves storage cost 
  • Keeps your stage clean 
  • Avoids reprocessing old files 

SCENARIO 3: Loading files into Snowflake while limiting how much data is processed in one run 

Problem: You may want to control batch size so that only files up to a certain total size are loaded at a time, while the remaining files are left for a later run. 

Parameter Used: 

  • SIZE_LIMIT = <num> (in bytes) 

What Happens: 

  • Snowflake loads files until the total size reaches the limit 

Behavior: 

  • Single small file → fully loaded 
  • Single large file → still fully loaded (no partial load) 
  • Multiple files → loaded until limit is reached; remaining files are skipped 

Example: If you try to bulk upsert File 1 = 10 MB, File 2 = 5 MB, and SIZE_LIMIT = 1,000,000 bytes (1 MB), Snowflake will load the first file completely and skip the second file. 

Why Use This: 

  • Helps control batch size 
  • Useful for testing or incremental loads 

SCENARIO 4: Exporting Snowflake data as a single output file. 

Problem: Some downstream systems expect only one file, so the export needs to produce a single file instead of multiple files. 

Parameter Used: 

  • SINGLE = TRUE 

What Happens: 

  • Snowflake writes all data into a single file 

Why Use This: 

  • Required when downstream systems expect a single file 
  • Best for small datasets (Note: Not recommended for large data as it reduces performance.)
  • Useful for testing or incremental loads 

 SCENARIO 5: Exporting large Snowflake data while controlling output file size. 

Problem: When unloading large volumes of data, you may want Snowflake to split the output into manageable file sizes instead of creating one very large file. 

Parameter Used: 

  • MAX_FILE_SIZE = <num> (in bytes) 

What Happens: 

  • Snowflake splits data into multiple files 
  • Each file tries to stay within the specified size 

Why files can be slightly LARGER: 

  • Snowflake does not split rows 
  • Formatting and metadata add extra size 

Why files can be SMALLER: 

  • Snowflake uses multiple workers (parallel processing) 
  • Data is split based on internal storage (micro-partitions) 

Important Understanding:

  • MAX_FILE_SIZE is a limit, not a strict rule 
  • Snowflake prioritizes performance over exact file size 

Example: If you set MAX_FILE_SIZE = 1,000,000 bytes (1 MB), you may still see files slightly larger than 1 MB and multiple smaller files due to parallel execution. 

You might see file names like Unload-File_0_6_3.csv. 

This means: 

  • 0 → Node ID 
  • 6 → Worker/Thread ID 
  • 3 → File sequence number 

This helps Snowflake manage files during parallel processing. 

Why Use This Parameter: 

  • Controls number of output files 
  • Balances performance vs file management 
  • Improves data transfer efficiency 

Final Thoughts 

Optional parameters in Snowflake are very powerful and help you optimize your integration flows based on your needs. 

Whether it is handling errors, managing storage, controlling file sizes, improving performance, these parameters give you better control over your data operations.

For more advanced and complete details on all available parameters, refer to the official Snowflake documentation for Bulk Upsert and Unload.

entry.title: Five Best Practices to Ensure a Smooth API Management Implementation
entry.content:
Over the past 8 years I have had the chance to work with many different API Management implementation projects, mostly with SAP API Management, but also with other APIM solutions like Azure, WSO2, Apigee, CA and Gravitee. In big organizations, there are often more than one APIM solutions in their landscape.

Now I would like to share some common mistakes observed in SAP API Management implementations. Hope this can help you to evaluate your organization’s API maturity level.

 

1) Using verify API key only to protect API

Verify API Key provides a simply way to protect and consume APIs. It is meant to be used in non-productive environments for prototype purposes. But still, I see many companies use it as the only mechanism to protect their APIs.

Action: Preferred alternatives for protecting APIs are mutual SSL or oAuth 2.0. If verify API key has to be used, then at least combine it with Access Control policy.

 

2) No proper control on request content and frequency

People tend to focus only on authentication aspect of APIs, but forget others possible vulnerabilities, like SQL injections and DOS attacks.

Action: Make sure to include the policies below in your baseline policy template:

  • XML / JSON Threat Protection: limits payload complexity to prevent memory overflow
  • Regular Expression Protection: prevents SQL injections
  • Spike Arrest: prevents DOS attacks
  • Quota: prevents overloading backend systems

 

3) Storing backend credentials in API Management

Usernames and passwords for the backend systems are stored in key value maps and letting APIs use them to gain access to backend. Doing this not only makes backend system lose the context of calling applications, but also sensitive information is being exchanged in emails, chats etc.

Action: Use principal propagation with the help of SAP Cloud Identity Service. Then if a business user is calling the API, their identity is propagated to a backend dialog user. And if a system is calling the API, its identity is propagated to a backend technical user. Different technical users in the backend can be used to represent different consuming applications. More importantly, there is no need to exchange or store user credentials in either consuming applications or API management.

 

4) Using Client Credentials grant types for all cases

OAuth has many different grant types, and it takes time to master them all. In general, use client credentials grant type for system-to-system integrations, and use authorization code / refresh token grant types for user-to-system integrations.

Action: Have a decision tree of when to use which grant types.

 

5) Using API Management as OAuth server

Although SAP API Management can issue access tokens, it is not a full-fledged authorization server. It also does not support the proper usage of scopes.

Action: Use SAP Cloud Identity Service, Azure Entra ID or Okta as OAuth 2.0 server.

 

Summary

API Management plays a key role in companies digital transformation journey. Mastering the tool is not difficult, but having good governance and smooth developer experience requires experience and dedication.

If you have tips for API Management, feel free to share them in the comment section!

entry.title: Joule for Consultants - Success Metrics
entry.content:
While we all have started using J4C , it is quite important to know how the success is meaured & evaluted in terms of J4C's adoption.https://www.sap.com/products/artificial-intelligence/ai-assistant/sap-consulting-capability.html 

Measuring the success of J4C requires establishing comprehensive Key Performance Indicators (KPIs) that evaluate user adoption, business impact, and technical performance. The measurement approach should cover KPIs that monitor influencing factors on user adoption both before and during the go-live phase, as well as KPIs to assess actual user adoption post go-live.

While J4C is still evolving but it has become one of the most important resources /tool for functional consultants as it caters to all types of projects like Greenfield , Upgrades, Migration , Conversion and many more.

I have tried to articulate possible metrics criteria in a tabular format.

Metric Category

Target Value

Measurement Period

Success Indicator

Consultant Adoption

80%+ active users

8-10 weeks during the project engagement

High engagement levels , Peer learning

Business Impact

15%+ productivity /operational efficiency gain

All project phases-implementation

Measurable efficiency improvements

Measurable efficiency improvements

99%+ accurate responses

Ongoing

Reliable tool performance

User Satisfaction

4.0+ rating (5-point scale) as an example

Monthly surveys

Positive user experience

entry.title: How to Distribute One G/L Account Balance to Multiple FS Items in SAP S4HANA Group Reporting
entry.content:
Introduction

In SAP S4HANA Group Reporting, it is common to encounter scenarios where a single G/L account contains balances that must be reported under multiple Financial Statement (FS) items. This is especially important for accurate consolidation and financial disclosures.

However, the standard system design only supports limited mapping relationships:

  • One G/L Account → One FS Item ✅
  • Multiple G/L Accounts → One FS Item ✅
  • One G/L Account → Multiple FS Items ❌ (not directly supported)

So what happens when business requirements demand splitting a single G/L account across multiple FS items?

This blog explains a practical and efficient solution using Substitution Rules in the Group Reporting Preparation Ledger (GRPL).

Business Scenario

Let’s consider a real-world example:

A single G/L account 52010010 – Other Expenses needs to be distributed across different cost centers:

  • 100101 – General & Administrative
  • 200301 – Selling & Distribution
  • 300401 – Cost of Goods Manufactured

Although the total balance is 50,000 USD, it is internally distributed based on cost centers.

Understanding the Data

1. Source and the GL Account balance

Source

GL Account

Amount in USD

S4HANA

52010010 – Other Expenses

50000

Table 1: Source data in ACDOCA Table

2. Source data as per the GL Account and Cost centers combination

SourceGL AccountCost centreAmount in USD
S4HANA52010010 - Other Expenses100101- General & Administrative10000
S4HANA52010010 - Other Expenses200301 - Selling & Distribution25000
S4HANA52010010 - Other Expenses300401- COGM                                                 15000
                                                                                 Total  -->

50000

Table 2: Source data with Cost center information

3. Target data

Cost Centre

Target FS Items

Target Amount in USD

100101

GA52010010 - General & Administrative

10000

200301

SD52010010 - Selling & Distribution

25000

300401

CG52010010 - Cost of Goods Manufactured

15000

Table 3: Target FS items based on the cost centers

 The Challenge

Standard Group Reporting configuration does not allow:

❌Assigning one G/L account to multiple FS items directly

This limitation prevents straightforward mapping for scenarios like the one above.

The Solution: Substitution Rules in GRPL

Once the Group Reporting Preparation Ledger (GRPL) is activated, you can use Substitution Rules to dynamically derive FS items during data release.

How It Works

At the time of data transfer From ACDOCA (Universal Journal) To ACDOCU (Group Reporting table) the system derives FS items based on:

  • G/L Account
  • Cost Centre
  • FS Item (target field)

This allows real-time transformation without additional postings.

Below is the Step-by-Step Configuration

Step 1: Create GR-Specific FS Items

Create separate FS items for each reporting requirement:

  • GA52010010 → General & Administrative
  • SD52010010 → Selling & Distribution
  • CG52010010 → Cost of Goods Manufactured

Step 2: Assign FS Items to P&L (Income Statement) Hierarchy

Update your Financial Statement hierarchy:

  • Place each FS item under the appropriate node
  • Ensure proper structure for income statement reporting

Note: You do NOT need traditional G/L-to-FS item mapping here. Substitution rules will handle the logic dynamically.

Step 3: Configure Substitution Rules

Use the “Substitution/Validation Rules – Journal Entries” Fiori app to configure the substitution rules.

Define substitution rules using:

  • G/L Account = 52010010
  • Cost Centre = specific values

Example logic:

  • If Cost Centre = 100101 → FS Item = GA52010010
  • If Cost Centre = 200301 → FS Item = SD52010010
  • If Cost Centre = 300401 → FS Item = CG52010010

In the substitution general Information configuration screen, enter the Substitution Rule Name and description and in the pre-condition and Substitution tabs, enter the required logic as shown below.

Note: for confidentiality sake, here I am not showing the cost centres IDs but enter the required information as per your requirements.

 Fig 1: Substitution configuration for the FS Item GA52010010

Similar to the above substitution configuration, write the other substitution rules for the remaining FS items

Below is the substitution configuration for the FS Items SD52010010

 Fig 2: Substitution configuration for the FS Item SD 52010010

Similarly, substitution rules can be created for the other FS items.

Step 4: Release Data

Execute the Release Universal Journal task from the Data Monitor to release data.

Final Result

  • Data from ACDOCA is transferred to ACDOCU
  • FS items are automatically derived based on substitution rules
  • No manual postings or allocation entries are required

✔ Real-time processing
✔ Clean data transformation
✔ Fully automated mapping

Key Benefits

Substitution rules allows you to:

  • Split one G/L account into multiple FS items
  • Use cost center or other dimensions for logic
  • Avoid manual journal entries
  • Enable real-time integration
  • Meet complex reporting requirements

Why Not Use Reporting Rules?

You might wonder: Can Reporting Rules handle this?

The answer is no, because:

  • Cost Center is not available as a derivation field
  • Reporting Rules cannot support this level of granularity

Therefore, Substitution Rules in GRPL are the correct and recommended approach.

Conclusion

Splitting a single G/L account across multiple FS items may seem challenging due to standard system limitations. However, by leveraging Substitution Rules within the Group Reporting Preparation Ledger, you can achieve a flexible, scalable, and real-time solution.

This approach not only aligns with SAP’s design principles but also ensures accuracy and efficiency in financial reporting.

Note: In my next blog, I’ll use another business scenario to explain how the substitution rule can be applied when there’s no direct solution to meet the requirement.

entry.title: Re: SAP Build Code – Technical Series – Feature Flags Service
entry.content:
Hi @AlexGiguere,

Thank you for all the inputs. From my side, all the links are working.

Regarding feature toggle and multi-tenancy concern, meanwhile I agree that the feature comes in the mtx package, it does not mean that this then will make you fully tied on a Multitenant application. As explained in the following CAP guide: https://cap.cloud.sap/docs/guides/extensibility/feature-toggles: These features can be assigned to individual SaaS customers (tenants), users, and requests and are then activated dynamically at runtime. So, it is not only tied to multitentant applications. Even more, the fact that you need to install mtx package, it does not mean that then you will activate multitenancy for your application. You can install the package: https://cap.cloud.sap/docs/guides/multitenancy/mtxs, and get the benefits of the feature toggles with the multitenancy flag deactivated.

Regarding the fact that feature toggle are evaluated at request level with the feature toggle plugin... You are right, but this does not mean that you cannot take the benefit of the feature flag service. If you check the CAP documentation and in my last answer on this blog post: "So, to add support for a specific feature toggles management you can add a simple Express.js middleware" -> https://cap.cloud.sap/docs/guides/extensibility/feature-toggles#feature-vector-providers

In my case, at the express middleware level, I am calling the feature flag service to get the status of the flag (This brings itself some latency counterparts, but then you can think about alternatives to store the answer of this service. But it should not be a huge concern if you test the application and see the response times). I am sharing with you again the code snippet that I used that makes the request to the feature flag service, to determine the final array of the req.features object:

cds.on('bootstrap', app => app.use(async (req, res, next) => {
   const userId = req.user?.id
   const response = await featureFlagService.evaluate('loyalty-discount', { identifier: userId })
   req.features = response.variation === 'true' ? ['loyalty-discount'] : []
   next()
}))

Regarding how to consume the service, I created a destination in my subaccount so it will resolve it.

Regarding the community plug in. Thank you for sharing it. It seems useful. I see that it is using a Redis instance to handle the flags and some new .yaml files should be introduced. I think that it depends on the experience around Redis from the developers/business users, then they will be able to manage them. But lets consider that many times the feature toggle itself it is not only for the developers that may consider the activation/deactivation of the new feature, maybe there are other personas (business users) involved around the decision that might like to have a look/interact with the flag, and maybe Redis is a bit difficult for them to understand/make a change. Anyway, I liked the approach of the community feature.

Kind regards,

Nata.

entry.title: Re: How to Implement Application Jobs which Automatically...
entry.content:
In S/4HANA Cloud, Public Edition, you can’t “close” a sales order just by setting a billing block;  that only prevents billing but does not change the order status. To truly close an order line, you need to apply a reason for rejection, and that only works if there are no subsequent documents (like delivery or billing) for that item.

Coming to custom field, usually it is not required, since SAP standard already provides the “Reason for Rejection” field at item level. You can leverage that in your custom logic instead of creating a new field, but not clear why you wanted to go with custom validation when you can straightaway set reason for rejection in sales order

entry.title: GRC Tuesdays: Leverage Mexico’s IMMEX program with SAP Global Trade Services and Origen Tech
entry.content:
In a world where fluctuations in tariffs and trade tensions between nations can impact the terms and conditions of international trade, being able to optimize costs by claiming duty relief from customs duties or applying for refunds on already paid ones, can significantly improve a company’s competitiveness and strengthen its market presence.

In some geographies, customs authorities allow companies to leverage special procedures providing some financial leeway for import and export processing, enabling cross-border manufacturing processes, and ultimately facilitating global supply chains.

When companies transfer products to special customs procedures, they may be able to reduce or avoid customs duties altogether or at least delay them until the goods in question enter the country or customs union of destination.

In exchange, however, customs authorities demand special diligence. Which is why they require authorizations for each of these customs procedures. In addition, authorities also demand special monitoring of the processes, including mandatory inventory management for these customs procedures.

One of the countries with such a program is Mexico.

 

Industria Manufacturera, Maquiladora y de Servicios de Exportación (IMMEX) program

 

Originally introduced as a special government program in 1965 to promote manufacturing investment on the United States-Mexican border, create employment opportunities, improve Mexico’s trade balance and drive technological development, the special customs procedure program in Mexico has continued to expand in usage when the country became a major manufacturing hub for companies looking to locate assembly operations outside the United States but still benefit from the North American Free Trade Agreement (NAFTA) – replaced in 2020 by the United States–Mexico–Canada Agreement (USMCA).

A new version of the program was launched in 2006 – the Industria Manufacturera, Maquiladora y de Servicios de Exportación (IMMEX) program, and “allows foreign manufacturers to import raw materials and components into Mexico, tax- and duty-free, under the condition that 100% of all finished goods will be exported out of Mexico within a government-mandated time frame” (North American Production Sharing). As of December 2025, the IMMEX program totalled 6,512 sites (plants and warehouses) and over 3 million jobs, thus making it not only a duty optimization program, but also a compelling operational strategy supporting supply chain resilience in North America. This includes a layer of protection since the USMCA defines a legal trade framework across North America.

As mentioned in the introduction, however, certain obligations must be met to be able to benefit from such a program.

In addition to eligibility criteria, for trade compliance specifically, there are 2 major obligations under IMMEX: Annex 24 and Annex 30. Together, they require organizations to:

  • Maintain an automated inventory control tracking temporarily imported goods
  • Ensure that goods are exported within the authorized import time frames

If these are not respected, the SAT (Servicio de Administración Tributaria) might require the company to pay applicable taxes, and could also impose fines and penalties that could even compromise a company’s IMMEX certification.

As you may already know, SAP Global Trade Service, edition for HANA supports inventory-managed special customs procedures in the European Union, but also in the United States Free Trade Zones and in China’s processing trade regime. But IMMEX was not yet supported.

Working with Origen Tech, this gap has now been covered and organizations can extend their SAP Global Trade Service solution with Origen Tech’s localization offering for Mexico to optimize import operations, pre-process import declarations, gain access to customs brokers and logistics operators, and enter advance payments and additional expenses in SAP Global Trade Services, with direct integration with the purchasing and accounting system in ERP (S/4 HANA or SAP ECC).

 

Who is Origen Tech

 

Founded in 2012, Origen Tech is a US-based SAP Gold Partner with expertise in SAP consulting, products and solution development focused on helping companies with their compliance and localization needs in the United States, Brazil  & Latin America. With headquarters located in Miami, Origen Tech also has presence in the main economies in Latin America.

A long-time partner for SAP Global Trade Services, but also SAP Document Reporting and Compliance, Origen Tech delivers localized country offerings and has built SAP partner extensions using the technology e-documents framework to those countries that are not on the planned SAP roadmap to enable real time e-invoicing and statutory reporting worldwide. This includes IMMEX in Mexico.

 

Why did SAP decide to partner in this area?

 

While SAP focuses on delivering core SAP Global Trade Services capabilities globally and full localization for chosen countries, we collaborate with trusted partners to extend the solution and offer customers localized functionalities tailored to their specific regional needs. 

When doing so, we focus on 5 main areas:

  • Time to market: so that the offering can be delivered rapidly when customers need it, and provide flexibility when regulators enforce new rules
  • Industry-specific: when requirements are global or mandatory for all companies, they will of course be included in the roadmap for the standard product but, for industry or geographic specifics, focused partners are more agile and closer to the need
  • Assurance: these extensions must follow SAP’s quality mandates to protect customers and maintain trust
  • Teamwork: since these enhancements are the result of a collaboration between 3 parties – the customer at the center, the partner who orchestrates and delivers the innovation, and SAP supporting the partner on technical aspects
  • State of the art: these enhancements natively integrate into SAP Global Trade Services since they are built on the same technology

 

What is the SAP Global Trade Services Mexico localization from Origen Tech?

 

By implementing Origen Tech's Mexico localization solution, businesses gain the ability to streamline and automate their cross-border supply chain processes using SAP Global Trade Services - enhancing global trade management, reducing costs, mitigating risks, and expediting customs clearance. Hence enabling companies to leverage the IMMEX program allowing for tax-free imports for re-exported products.

Dedicated SAP Global Trade Services add-on features delivered with this localization include:

 

o   Material classification under Mexico’s Commercial Identification Number (NICO) scheme

o   Data stage integration to run analytical reports, and compare official custom forms (pedimentos) against system generated declarations in SAP Global Trade Services, automatically identifying discrepancies

o   Total cost of duty imports determination, including planned and unplanned costs

o   Facilitated communication with customs brokers, including the electronic file (expediente electrónico)

 

 Additionally, this localization allows direct integration with the Mexican authorities’ foreign trade receipt system (Ventanilla Única de Comercio Exterior Mexicana, or VUCEM) to obtain the final import documentation.

 

What benefits can organizations – and their trade compliance department, expect?

 

If helping companies optimize customs duties by avoiding “worst case” determination and strengthen their market presence by improving their product competitiveness is of course the ultimate benefit, to achieve this objective companies must ensure compliance with the mandatory requirements. In this aspect, this localization helps trade compliance departments:

 

Manage and apply trade preferences and agreements

 

Determine eligibility for preferential tariff rates based on trade agreements

 

Automate calculations of applicable preferences during import/export

 

Facilitate compliance with trade preference regulations for cost optimization

 

Interested in learning more?

 

Then feel free to view the recording of the live co-innovation session between Origen Tech and SAP on the theme SAP Global Trade Services Localisation for Mexico and Brazil delivered at the SAP for Global Trade and Tax Conference organized by TAC Insights in Rome in November 2025.

And, if you are looking for more details on IMMEX and how Origen Tech can extend your SAP Global Trade Service edition for HANA to meet the specific custom requirements, then have a look at the dedicated page: IMMEX program for Mexico

entry.title: Re: The Orders with Item Level Delivery Block are not ref...
entry.content:
In S/4HANA Cloud, Public Edition, you can’t “close” a sales order just by setting a billing block — that only prevents billing but does not change the order status. To truly close an order line, you need to apply a reason for rejection, and that only works if there are no subsequent documents (like delivery or billing) for that item.

Coming to custom field, usually it is not required, since SAP standard already provides the “Reason for Rejection” field at item level. You can leverage that in your custom logic instead of creating a new field, but not clear why you wanted to go with custom validation when you can straightaway set reason for rejection in sales order.

If you implement custom validation that forces delivery‑blocked items into credit exposure, you risk distorting the customer’s credit position. The exposure figure would no longer represent the actual deliverable risk, leading to:

Overstated exposure: blocked items inflate the customer’s credit usage even though they cannot be delivered or billed.

Inconsistent reporting: finance and risk teams may see mismatches between exposure values and real open order values.

Decision errors: credit approvals, limits, and monitoring could be based on inaccurate data, undermining compliance and audit reliability.

entry.title: Re: SAP Datasphere: Configuration Differences (BDC Integr...
entry.content:
Hello @quocdt5 ,


The main difference is that standalone SAP Datasphere is used as an independent service for data integration, modeling, warehousing, federation, and virtualization, while SAP Datasphere integrated into SAP Business Data Cloud (BDC) includes the same core capabilities plus additional BDC features. [crescenseinc.com], [learning.sap.com]
When Datasphere is part of SAP BDC, it also comes with SAP-managed content, automated data integration, One Domain Model (ODM), SAP BW integration, Databricks/AI capabilities, and a more unified SaaS experience, which reduces some of the manual setup compared to the standalone version. [learning.sap.com]
So, in short, the base Datasphere configuration remains largely similar, but the BDC-integrated version provides extra prebuilt services, governance, and integration options. [learning.sap.com], [crescenseinc.com]


Best regards,
Sravan Aleshwaram

entry.title: Re: How to implement timeout for synchronous RFC calls fr...
entry.content:
Hello @udit_dahima-18 ,


For synchronous RFC (sRFC / SAP JCo), the SAP-side controls are mainly general dialog/runtime and server resource protections, not a dedicated per-call sRFC execution timeout. The key runtime guardrail is rdisp/max_wprun_time for dialog processing, while SAP’s rdisp/rfc* parameters are documented for qRFC / parallel RFC and do not apply to synchronous RFC. 


Because sRFC waits for the remote response, it is best suited for short request/response operations. For long-running SAPCAR logic, the safer SAP-aligned design is to avoid synchronous RFC and use an asynchronous pattern such as bgRFC (or tRFC/qRFC where required). SAP documents bgRFC as a genuine asynchronous communication method that supports reliable processing, and RFC variants documentation positions tRFC as the predecessor of bgRFC.

Recommended approach:

Keep sRFC only for short, deterministic calls. 
Move long-running processing to bgRFC / tRFC / qRFC or a background job, and return a request ID / status to the caller instead of holding the connection open.
If needed, use SAP’s server resource protection (rdisp/server_resource_limit / rdisp/resource_limit) to protect the system under high load, noting that this is admission control, not a true per-call cancellation timeout.


Best regards,
Sravan Aleshwaram

entry.title: Re: profit center is not coming in both the line
entry.content:
Why profit center is missing is because, Profit center is derived either from cost objects (like cost center, internal order) or from account assignment logic (OKB9, substitution, document splitting). For balance sheet accounts (like clearing accounts), profit center is not automatically derived unless document splitting is active or a substitution rule is in place. Since you have confirmed document splitting is not active and no cost center is provided, the clearing line will remain without a profit center.

To achieve your requirement, create a substitution rule to populate profit center on the clearing line based on contract attributes or company code logic. Alternatively, you can go with custom validation with available BAdI in your system, to derive profit center for balance sheet items.

entry.title: Custom Business Configurations (F4579): Implementing an editable tree view (hierarchy)
entry.content:
Introduction

The Custom Business Configurations (CUBCO) app serves as an entry point to the Business Configuration Maintenance Object (SMBC) provided by custom applications or partners.
This blog explains how to expose the hierarchical structure of a CDS hierarchy as an editable tree view in the CUBCO app.
For this purpose the SMBC object now supports tree table as table type.

For more details on hierarchies and more detailed examples and explanations, see the links above. The purpose of this blog is to demonstrate the tree view capability of the CUBCO app using a simple example.

This blog is relevant for

  • SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition (2025, SAP Note 3687833, SAP UI5 1.136.12)
  • SAP BTP ABAP environment (2605)

Further reading:

  • Related blog posts
  • Learn how you can use ABAP technology to develop innovative applications and business solutions across SAP’s portfolio on SAP Learning Site.

Example scenario

In the configuration table ZDEMO_CC, the company codes are stored in a parent-child relationship to organize the corporate structure in a recursive hierarchy.

@EndUserText.label : 'Company Code'
@AbapCatalog.enhancement.category : #NOT_EXTENSIBLE
@AbapCatalog.tableCategory : #TRANSPARENT
@AbapCatalog.deliveryClass : #C
@AbapCatalog.dataMaintenance : #ALLOWED
define table zdemo_cc {
  key client            : abap.clnt not null;
  key company_code      : abap.numc(4) not null;
  country               : land1;
  parent_company        : abap.numc(4);
  last_changed_at       : abp_lastchange_tstmpl;
  local_last_changed_at : abp_locinst_lastchange_tstmpl;
}

Implement editable tree view

First we use the ADT wizard to generate a Business Configuration Maintenance Object for this table. In the wizard, select the option "Create Projection Layer". Apart from this, leave settings as they are and complete the wizard.

Add recursive associations

Add associations _ParentCompany and _Company to generated CDS view ZI_COMPANYCODE. Save and activate.

@EndUserText.label: 'Company Code'
@AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #MANDATORY
define view entity ZI_CompanyCode
  as select from zdemo_cc
  association         to parent ZI_CompanyCode_S as _CompanyCodeAll on $projection.SingletonID = _CompanyCodeAll.SingletonID
  association of many to one ZI_CompanyCode      as _ParentCompany  on $projection.ParentCompany = _ParentCompany.CompanyCode
  association of many to many ZI_CompanyCode     as _Company        on $projection.CompanyCode = _Company.ParentCompany

{
  key company_code          as CompanyCode,
      @Semantics.address.country: true
      country               as Country,
      parent_company        as ParentCompany,
      @Semantics.systemDateTime.lastChangedAt: true
      last_changed_at       as LastChangedAt,
      @Semantics.systemDateTime.localInstanceLastChangedAt: true
      local_last_changed_at as LocalLastChangedAt,
      1                     as SingletonID,
      _CompanyCodeAll,
      _ParentCompany,
      _Company
}

Create CDS hierarchy

Create a new CDS Data Definition ZI_COMPANYCODEHN. Save and activate.

@AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #NOT_REQUIRED
define hierarchy ZI_COMPANYCODEHN
  with parameters
    P_SingletonID : abap.int1
  as parent child hierarchy(
    source ZI_CompanyCode
    child to parent association _ParentCompany
    directory _CompanyCodeAll filter by
      SingletonID = $parameters.P_SingletonID
    start where
      ParentCompany is initial
    siblings order by
      CompanyCode ascending
  )
{
  key CompanyCode,
      ParentCompany,
      SingletonID
}

Add recursive hierarchy to CDS definition

Add the recursive hierarchy and the redirections to the generated projection CDS view ZC_COMPANYCODE

@EndUserText.label: 'Maintain Company Code'
@AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #MANDATORY
@Metadata.allowExtensions: true
@OData.hierarchy.recursiveHierarchy:[{ entity.name: 'ZI_COMPANYCODEHN' }]
define view entity ZC_CompanyCode
  as projection on ZI_CompanyCode
{
  key CompanyCode,
      Country,
      ParentCompany,
      LastChangedAt,
      @Consumption.hidden: true
      LocalLastChangedAt,
      @Consumption.hidden: true
      SingletonID,
      _CompanyCodeAll : redirected to parent ZC_CompanyCode_S,
      _ParentCompany  : redirected to ZC_CompanyCode,
      _Company        : redirected to ZC_CompanyCode
}

Adjust behavior definition

Add the following to the definition of entity CompanyCode in the generated behavior definition ZI_COMPANYCODE_S. Save and activate.

  association _ParentCompany
  {
    with draft;
    link action linkCompany;
    unlink action unlinkCompany;
  }

  association _Company { with draft; }

  instance hierarchy ZI_COMPANYCODEHN
  {
    ascending association _ParentCompany;
    descending association _Company { with cascading delete; }
  }

Extend the behavior projection ZC_COMPANYCODE_S accordingly. Save and activate.

define behavior for ZC_CompanyCode alias CompanyCode
use etag
{
  use update;
  use delete;

  use association _CompanyCodeAll { with draft; }
  use association _ParentCompany
  {
    with draft;
    link action linkManager;
    unlink action unlinkManager;
  }
  use association _Company { with draft; }

}

Set table type

Open the generated SMBC object in ADT. Expand the Table Settings section, select the row for entity CompanyCode and click Edit.
Change Table Type to Tree Table and Table Creation Mode to New Page . Save and activate.

Result

You can maintain your company structure while using the hierarchy features of SAP Fiori elements, such as: Collapse/expand tree, cut and paste nodes, or drag and drop nodes.

Further considerations

  • You can set the ParentCompany field as read-only. The user can still change the parent company, for example, using drag and drop functionality. However, you cannot modify the ParentCompany field with ABAP EML, only with ABAP SQL.
  • You can remove the ParentCompany field from the tree table by removing the corresponding UI.lineItem from the metadata extension. It should be sufficient to display the field on the object page as the parent is specified by the visible hierarchy structure.
  • If the ParentCompany field is enabled for editing, you can provide a value help and implement a validation for create and update to prevent an inconsistent hierarchy. However, a validation does not prevent the update of the draft version. When you reload the app, the entity with an invalid parent disappears. Deletion is configured as cascading, so no additional validation is required for the deletion process. 
  • Instead, consider using a precheck to prevent inconsistent hierarchy changes.

entry.title: Troubleshooting Hidden Header Data in UDM_DISPUTE after Kernel Updates - S4HANA FI/FSCM
entry.content:
Troubleshooting Hidden Header Data in UDM_DISPUTE after Kernel Updates

If your users have recently complained that the Header Data in their Dispute Management cases has "vanished" after a system maintenance window, you aren't alone. This UI glitch specifically affects the Fiori Launchpad (SAP GUI for HTML) version of transaction UDM_DISPUTE.

The Symptom

The issue typically manifests when a user navigates into a specific collection case. After selecting "Look for Dispute Case" and double-clicking a Case ID, the expected Header Data area appears empty or hidden behind the application frame .

Curiously, this behavior is environment-specific:

  • SAP GUI for Windows: Functions perfectly.
  • Fiori Launchpad/WebGUI: The Header is obscured.
  • Workaround: Scrolling to the bottom frame sometimes triggers a "snap" that forces the data to reappear.

Technical Root Cause: The "Scroll-Into-View" Conflict

This isn't a data loss issue; it's a rendering conflict between the application container and the browser's native accessibility features.

  1. Fixed Container Sizes: The application’s deepest container is designed with fixed dimensions that sometimes exceed the active browser window size.
  2. Programmatic Focus: By design, the application sets the cursor focus to the first cell of the table.
  3. The Trigger: Because this focused cell is technically "off-screen" according to the browser's calculations, the browser executes a native scroll-into-view action. This shifts the entire page content upward, effectively pushing the Header Data section off the top of the visible area (hiding it behind the frame).

This behavior has been specifically noted following Kernel updates, which can alter how the WebGUI interprets container scaling and focus.

The Solution

To resolve this, you need to address the underlying Case Management (SCASE) Web application logic that handles container sizing.

1. Primary Fix: SAP Note 3680679

The official resolution is to implement SAP Note 3680679 (Header Data is not visible in the Case Management (SCASE) Web application). This note provides the necessary corrections to ensure the container sizing and focus logic play nicely with modern browser scrolling.

2. Affected Products

This fix is relevant across several SAP environments, as the core Case Management component

(BC-SRV-CM) is widely utilized:

  • SAP S/4HANA (All versions)
  • SAP NetWeaver (All versions)
  • SAP Business Technology Platform

Summary

When UI elements go missing in Fiori-wrapped transactions like UDM_DISPUTE, the culprit is often a misalignment between the legacy SAP GUI design and the browser's viewport handling. If you've recently patched your Kernel, ensure SAP Note 3680679 is part of your remediation plan to keep your Header Data visible and your Finance team happy.

The alternative to manual re-creation is to use abapGit plug in ADT which reduces the manual effort.

entry.title: Re: FIFO Behavior for Whse Managed Storage Locations for...
entry.content: To meet the requirement, enable HU management for serial‑managed items. This way, each HU retains its own GR date, and FIFO can be based on that date. If HU management is not acceptable, you’d need a custom enhancement/report to track the original GR date and drive picking logic accordingly, but that’s outside standard Public Cloud behavior.

entry.title: Re: Mass Deletion of PRs with Deleted PO Items (S/4HANA P...
entry.content:
In Fiori app F1048 - Manage Purchase Requisitions – Professional, use filters (e.g., “Follow‑on Document Deleted”) and mass actions to set PR items to “Closed” or “Rejected”. Public Cloud provides mass processing apps for PRs where you can apply rejection reasons or deletion flags in bulk. Alternatively, you can define a business rule so that when a PO item is deleted, the linked PR item is automatically set to “Closed” or “Rejected.” This is done via Custom Logic (BAdI) in the In‑App Extensibility framework, not by altering core tables.

If you try to force PR closure through custom validation or backend manipulation, you risk:

  • Inconsistent demand planning (MRP will misinterpret requirements).
  • Audit issues (PR lifecycle not traceable).
  • Upgrade conflicts (custom code breaks with quarterly Cloud updates).

entry.title: Getting Services Right from the Start: Why the Product Type Group Should Be Assignable in the IR
entry.content:
Getting Services Right from the Start:

Why the Product Type Group Should Be Assignable in the Purchasing Info Record

Accurate classification of materials and services is essential for smooth procurement and follow‑on processes in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud. Especially in production‑related procurement, correctly distinguishing between materials and services is not optional—it is a prerequisite for compliant and efficient execution.

Yet in real customer scenarios, this distinction is not always preserved automatically. A critical gap exists around the Product Type Group when external services are procured via Purchasing Info Records.

________________________________________________________________________________________________________

The Business Scenario: External Processing in Production Orders

A typical business case looks like this:

  1. A Production Order is created
  2. An Operation for external processing is defined
  3. This operation uses a Purchasing Info Record
  4. A Purchase Requisition item is generated automatically
  5. A Purchase Order is created based on the requisition

At first glance, this end‑to‑end process appears fully integrated and automated. However, an important detail is missing along the way.

________________________________________________________________________________________________________

The Gap Today: Product Type Group Is Defaulted Incorrectly

The issue arises at the Purchase Requisition level:

  • The automatically created Purchase Requisition item does not carry a Product Type Group
  • As a result, when the Purchase Order is created, the system defaults the Product Type Group to:
    • 1 – Material

For external services, this default is incorrect.

Services must be handled differently from materials, both functionally and legally. By defaulting to “Material”, the system places the document on the wrong processing path from the very beginning.

________________________________________________________________________________________________________

Why This Is a Critical Issue for Service Procurement

The incorrect Product Type Group has direct downstream consequences:

  • Service‑specific processes are not triggered
  • The Service Entry Sheet process is not initiated correctly
  • Additional manual correction is required
  • The risk of posting and compliance errors increases

In other words:
Although the business process clearly involves services, the system treats them as materials—forcing users to intervene manually.

This contradicts the goal of automated, guided processes in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud.

________________________________________________________________________________________________________

Root Cause: Missing Product Type Group in the Purchasing Info Record

The underlying reason for this issue is straightforward:

  • The Purchasing Info Record does not allow maintenance of the Product Type Group
  • Therefore, the Purchase Requisition created from it lacks this essential classification
  • The Purchase Order inherits an incorrect default

Without a way to define whether the Info Record represents a material or a service, the system cannot make the right decision automatically.

________________________________________________________________________________________________________

The Proposed Improvement: Extend the Purchasing Info Record

The improvement request proposes a clear and targeted enhancement:

Allow the Product Type Group to be maintained in the Purchasing Info Record and transferred automatically to the Purchase Requisition and Purchase Order.

With this enhancement in place:

  • External services can be clearly marked as Product Type Group 2 – Service
  • Materials remain correctly classified as Product Type Group 1 – Material
  • The correct processing path is selected automatically

A small master data extension would eliminate a recurring operational issue.

________________________________________________________________________________________________________

Business Benefits of This Improvement

Introducing the Product Type Group into the Purchasing Info Record delivers multiple benefits:

  • Correct service processing from the start
    Service entry sheets and related follow‑up processes are triggered as intended.

  • Reduced manual effort
    No need for corrections during Purchase Order creation.

  • Higher data quality and consistency
    Classification reflects the true business context.

  • Improved compliance and process reliability
    Especially important for service procurement in production environments.

  • Enhanced user experience
    Users can rely on the system instead of compensating for missing logic.

________________________________________________________________________________________________________

Call to Action: Support Better Service Handling in Procurement

This improvement request addresses a real, recurring issue in production‑related service procurement. If your organization uses external processing or service procurement via Production Orders and Purchasing Info Records, I encourage you to:

👉 Please Vote to Idea # 369894 on SAP Influence and help move this enhancement forward.

entry.title: Hello SAP Community! My name is Amrutha Mothukuri and my journey begins here
entry.content:
Hello SAP Community!

My name is Amrutha Mothukuri and I’m a currently pursuing my career in Bachelor of Technology at Chalapathi Institute of Engineering and Technology

I come from a background in Bachelor of Technology , and I have developed a strong interest in SAP technologies, especially in areas like Sales and Distribution (SD), SAPUI5, and business process integration. I am passionate about learning how businesses operate and how SAP solutions help streamline processes efficiently.

As part of the Hello SAP Community Initiative, I’m excited to share my experience. You can read more about the initiative in  Hello SAP Community Initiative , which gives the full background and context.

What is your professional life dream?

My professional dream is to become an SAP expert who can design and implement efficient business solutions. I aspire to work on real-time SAP projects, contribute to digital transformation, and help organizations optimize their operations using SAP technologies. 

During my SAP learning journey, I focused mainly on the functional side by working on business-oriented scenarios related to the Order-to-Cash (O2C) process in SAP Sales and Distribution (SD). Through this, I gained a strong understanding of key concepts such as enterprise structure, customer and material master data, pricing procedures, and the complete sales order lifecycle, which helped me connect theoretical knowledge with real-time business processes in SAP S/4HANA.

In addition, I have completed certifications in Sales and Distribution (SD), Generative AI, and End-to-End Business Processes, which have enhanced my understanding of how modern technologies and SAP solutions work together to support business operations. These learning experiences have not only strengthened my functional knowledge but also increased my interest in exploring how intelligent technologies like AI can further transform enterprise systems.

Where do you see yourself in 5 years?

In the next five years, I see myself working as an experienced SAP consultant with strong technical and functional knowledge. I aim to gain expertise in SAP modules and be part of large-scale enterprise implementations.

What is your hobby?

My hobbies include learning new technologies, coding, and exploring SAP tools. I also enjoy listening music, reading books ,dancing and exploring about new technologies, which helps me stay creative and refreshed.

What is the best lesson your parents taught you?

The most valuable lesson my parents taught me is hard work and consistency. They always encouraged me to stay focused on my goals and never give up, no matter the challenges.

My SAP TechEd Bangalore Experience

This year I had the opportunity to attend SAP TechEd Bangalore 2025, where I explored the latest innovations in SAP technologies. It was an incredible experience to learn from experts, attend sessions, and network with professionals from across the globe.

 

And now let’s hear from you!

What is your professional life dream?

I would love to hear your thoughts please share your answers in the comments below!

entry.title: Re: Automatic payment processing using DME for Internatio...
entry.content:
Good day @Rama21 

Thank you for your question.

Based on what SAP offers in the Public Cloud, your question would have to be addressed in two ways. Let me clarify:

First let me address your own question

Question: Is it possible in SAP Hana S4 Public cloud to automatically determine and use the correct Vendor bank account during the payment based on Currency?

Answer: The Public Cloud does not offer a feature that automatically switches among several supplier bank accounts purely by currency alone. The reason is, in Public Cloud, automatic payment processing does not support currency-based bank determination for your own house bank account, because Set Up Bank Determination for Payment Transaction (SSCUI 101045) is maintained by payment method and payment currency.

The distinction and possibilities attached to your question:

  • If your question is: Can the Public Cloud automatically choose our own paying bank account by currency?

Answer: Yes, that is standard and you configure it in SSCUI 101045 with payment method and currency.

2671294 - No Valid Payment Method Found - SAP for Me

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/4603118feda9495892abadbe8f8c3d14/3c0bb62cebd04e3bb53db9cb37ac355f.html?locale=en-US

  • If your question is: Can the Public Cloud automatically choose the correct supplier bank account only from currency when the supplier has multiple bank accounts?

Answer: That would be outside the standard rule. The safer approach for you is to maintain supplier bank details correctly and use Partner Bank Type when a specific supplier bank account must be selected.

 

Practically, I would recommend for your scenario to treat the standard design as follows:

  • Configure currency-based determination for your house bank in SSCUI 101045.
  • Use Maintain Business Partner to maintain all supplier bank accounts and use Partner Bank Type to identify the intended supplier bank account when you need to.

Here are references for more guidance

3444334 - Error "Customer/vendor does not have suitable bank details for payment method &" - SAP for Me

2671294 - No Valid Payment Method Found - SAP for Me

 

The next step is to verify two things separately in your tenant: First, that the correct house bank is being selected for each currency in the payment proposal, and second, that the supplier has all bank details maintained in Automatic Payment Transactions with clear Partner Bank Type values.

 

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Hello SAP Community! My name is Appikatla Venkata Murali Krishna
entry.content:
Hello SAP Community!

My name is Appikatla Venkata Murali krishna, and I am currently an aspiring SAP MM Consultant. I have completed my studies and have been actively building my skills in SAP S/4HANA with a focus on Materials Management (MM).

I have worked on real-time scenarios like procurement processes, freight vendor integration, and inventory management, which helped me understand how businesses manage materials efficiently using SAP. I am passionate about learning new SAP technologies and improving my functional knowledge to grow in this field.

As part of the Hello SAP Community Initiative, I’m excited to share my experience. You can read more about the initiative in Hello SAP Community Initiative , which gives the full background and context.

What is your professional life dream?

My professional dream is to become a successful SAP MM Consultant working on global implementation projects. I aim to contribute to organizations by optimizing procurement processes and improving supply chain efficiency using SAP solutions.

What do you imagine is the future of your topic (SAP, AI, Cloud)?

I believe the future of SAP lies in the deep integration of AI and Cloud within business processes, especially in areas like procurement and supply chain management. SAP S/4HANA Cloud, combined with Generative AI and predictive analytics, will transform how businesses make decisions.

For example, in a procurement scenario, instead of manually creating purchase requisitions based on stock levels, AI can automatically:

  • Predict material demand using historical data
  • Suggest the best vendor based on price, delivery performance, and past history
  • Auto-create purchase requisitions and even purchase orders
  • Detect anomalies such as unusual pricing or duplicate invoices

In SAP MM, this means processes like inventory management, vendor selection, and invoice verification will become more intelligent and automated. AI can also help in physical inventory by identifying discrepancies and suggesting corrections in real time.

Generative AI will further enhance user experience by allowing users to interact with SAP systems using natural language. For example, a user could simply ask:

"Show me the materials with low stock and create a purchase order for the best vendor" — and the system can execute it.

Overall, the future will shift from manual execution to intelligent decision-making, where SAP professionals will focus more on process optimization and strategy rather than routine tasks.

What is your hobby?

My hobbies include learning new technologies, exploring SAP scenarios, and improving my technical knowledge. I also enjoy watching tech-related content and staying updated with industry trends.

What was the most fun project you ever participated in and why?

One of the most interesting projects I worked on was Freight Vendor Integration in SAP MM. In this project, I learned how to configure condition types like FRB1, manage multiple vendors (material and freight), and handle invoice verification. It was fun because it connected real-world logistics with SAP configuration.

What do you enjoy most in your studies/work and why?

What I enjoy most is understanding real-time business processes and configuring them in SAP. It is exciting to see how a business requirement is translated into a system solution. Especially in SAP MM, I enjoy working on purchase orders, inventory, and vendor management.

My SAP TechEd Bangalore Experience

During SAP TechEd Bangalore 2025, I gained valuable insights into how SAP is evolving with modern technologies. Some of my key learnings include:

  • SAP is strongly focusing on AI-driven automation, especially with tools like SAP Joule (AI Copilot), which helps users perform tasks faster using natural language
  • Learned how SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP) plays a key role in integrating AI, data, and analytics across different modules
  • Understood the importance of clean core strategy in S/4HANA for better system performance and easier upgrades
  • Saw real-world use cases where AI is used in procurement to automate vendor selection and improve decision-making
  • Gained awareness of how cloud-based SAP solutions are becoming the standard for future implementations

One important takeaway for me was that future SAP consultants need not only functional knowledge but also an understanding of AI and cloud technologies. This motivated me to continue learning and upgrading my skills to stay relevant in the evolving SAP ecosystem.

And now let’s hear from you!

Where do you see yourself in 5 years?

I would love to hear your thoughts and experiences please share them in the comments!

entry.title: Hello SAP community my name is Vishwa Bharadhwaj S P I'm excited to start my journey as a student
entry.content:
Hello SAP community, my name is Vishwa Bharadhwaj S P. I’m currently a student, and over time I’ve been exploring my interest in technology and trying to understand how things work beyond just what’s taught in class. My journey as a student hasn’t been perfect, but it has helped me realize the importance of learning on my own and improving step by step.  I spend time building my knowledge through different resources and trying to get better at what I do. I’m still figuring things out, but I’m focused on growing and building a strong career over time.

Here are the five questions I would like to answer as a a part of the "Hello SAP Community" initiative 

1. What is your professional life dream?

I want to build a career in technology where I’m actually useful, not just doing tasks for the sake of it. I’ve always been curious about how systems work, so I want to be in a place where I can solve real problems and see the impact of what I’m doing. I don’t have everything figured out yet, but I know I want to keep learning, get better at what I do, and eventually reach a level where people trust my work.

2. Where do you see yourself in 5 years?

If I’m being honest, I don’t have a perfect picture. But I do see myself more confident and experienced than I am now. I want to be working on real projects, not just learning basics, and actually understanding what I’m doing instead of guessing. I’d like to be in a role where I’m improving consistently and not feeling stuck.

3. What is your hobby?

Most of the time, I end up exploring new technologies or learning something online. It’s not always planned, I just go down random topics and try to understand how things work. I also play chess quite a bit. It helps me slow down and think properly instead of rushing decisions. Sometimes I lose badly, but that’s part of it. Going to the gym is another thing I’ve been consistent with. It clears my head. It’s one of the few places where progress feels straightforward, you show up, you work, and over time you see change.

4. Which 4 things do you absolutely want to achieve in life?

I want to build a career where I feel stable and know what I’m doing.
I want to keep learning instead of becoming comfortable and stagnant.
I want to do something useful with what I learn, not just work for a salary.
I want to reach a point where my journey makes sense to someone else who’s trying to figure things out.

5. What is the best lesson your parents taught you?

They didn’t really sit me down and explain big life lessons, but I’ve seen how they handled things. They kept going even when things weren’t easy. That stuck with me. So for me, the lesson is simple, keep doing the work even when you don’t feel like it. Don’t depend too much on motivation. Just stay consistent and things slowly start making sense.

6.How do you choose which area to focus on in SAP as a student?

 I think it depends on your background. Since I come from mechanical engineering, I find SAP Material Management more relatable because it connects with materials, inventory, and supply processes that I’ve already studied in theory. SAP MM feels like digital side of the same process where everything is organized, tracked, planned and optimized

My SAP Inside Track 2026 Experience 

Attended SAP Inside Track 2026 with my friends, and honestly that made the experience better. We discussed a lot after each session, and it helped me understand things more clearly instead of just listening and moving on. I had great insights on Gen Ai and Applied research and the clarity of the content was perfect.

This was inside the SAP Labs Innovation Park. Seeing the model and how everything is structured gave me a better idea of how large-scale systems and environments are planned. It felt more practical than just learning concepts in theory.

The campus itself left an impression. It did not feel like a typical place, it felt like a space where actual innovation happens. Being there made things feel more real and gave me a better sense of where I want to reach.

Now let's hear it from you.

One thing I'm still thinking about after the session is

How are AI agents being practically integrated into enterprise systems, and what challenges come up during implementation?
